original_abstract: The efficient electro-oxidation of alcohols to CO2 via the cleavage of carbon-carbon (C-C) bonds remains a significant hurdle in the commercialization of high-energy-density direct alcohol fuel cells. In this work, we report the synthesis of ultrathin (2.3 nm) core/shell Au/Pt bimetallic nanowires (NWs) featuring stepped Pt clusters deposited along a stepped Au NW core. These low-coordinated surface sites demonstrate exceptional catalytic performance in alkaline media, which is highly sensitive to the Au/Pt atomic ratio. Specifically, Au1.0/Pt0.2 NWs—which possess the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (~75%)—achieve an unprecedented mass activity of 196.9 A/mgPt for the ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR). Beyond ethanol, this catalyst architecture proves highly effective for the oxidation of other primary alcohols, including methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. Using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy and in situ infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy, we identified key reaction intermediates and demonstrated that the synergy between the stretched Pt lattice and the stepped surface morphology effectively suppresses CO* poisoning while facilitating OH* adsorption. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations corroborate these findings, revealing that the under-coordinated Au-Pt facets significantly lower the energy barriers for C-C bond cleavage and CO* desorption. This study introduces a robust strategy for surface-morphology engineering in bimetallic catalysts to drive complex multi-electron oxidation processes for renewable energy applications.
humanized_abstract: The electro-oxidation of alcohols to carbon dioxide with direct scission of carboncarbon (C-C) bond over catalyst remains a major challenge in achieving the technological advancement of direct alcohol fuel cells (DAFCs) for large-scale energy storage and conversion. In this study, ultrathin core/shell structure Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) composed of stepped Pt clusters on a stepped Au single-crystal nanocore (thickness 2.3 nm) were obtained. The low-coordinated surface structure catalyzed the alkaline ethanol electro-oxidation reaction that was sensitive to the atomic ratio of Au to Pt. Interestingly, the mass activity of Au1.0/Pt0.2 catalyst to ethanol electro-oxidation reaction reached up to 196.9 A/mgPt. Apart from ethanol, other primary alcohols like methanol, n-propanol and ethylene glycol were also catalytically oxidized efficiently over this Au/Pt catalyst. The occurrence of various electro-oxidation reaction intermediates have been identified using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and in situ infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy (IRRAS). The stretched lattice of Pt as well as the inherent structure of the stepped surface effectively hindered the strong poisoning of catalyst by CO*, while enhanced the adsorption of the active OH* species in the alkaline solution for catalyzing the ethanol electro-oxidation efficiently. The energy barriers of C-C bond scission and desorption of CO* are reduced on under-coordinated Au-Pt facets, which was simulated by the density functional theory (DFT). A strategy for the design and preparation of catalysts toward large-scale fuel cell application has been established by modulating the surface structure for the multi-electron transformations.
